results.possibleIdentity: Eric Martin (likely Eric C. Martin) was an architect, designer, and filmmaker who was active in the late 1960s and early 1970s, particularly within the intersection of art and technology. In 1968, while a student of architecture at Columbia University in New York City, he collaborated with artist Robin Parkinson to create the 'Toy-Pet Plexi-Ball,' a kinetic sculpture that responded to light and sound. This work won a first prize in a competition organized by Experiments in Art and Technology (E.A.T.) and was featured in the landmark Museum of Modern Art (MoMA) exhibition 'The Machine as Seen at the End of the Mechanical Age' (1968). Martin was also a significant contributor to the design and documentation of the Pepsi Pavilion for Expo '70 in Osaka, Japan, a major multidisciplinary project spearheaded by E.A.T. He later served as a faculty member at Harvard University's Carpenter Center for the Visual Arts (Department of Visual and Environmental Studies), where he collaborated with MIT researchers on early interactive computer-mediated animation systems, such as the Genesys system.
